item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this SCP, +1.

My one critique is that I think the article should at least mention how anomalous it is to find "proto-Latin" writing from the Upper Paleolithic, so it doesn't just seem like a lack of linguistics knowledge or sloppy writing or both. I presume the author intended it to be anomalous?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am like four years late to this, but for reference, after his death, Guan Yu was deified (as pointed out in the footnotes) in the Sui dynasty, and is still worshipped in both Buddhism and Chinese folk religion.

But more or less, yeah.

item.title: Re: Unresolved
item.content:encoded: As it is, fixing the numbers would be a stylistic edit, so per site policy it's up to the author to make those changes. You can try sending a wikidot message directly to Hornby, but there's no guarantee of a response.

item.title: Unresolved
item.content:encoded:
I know this comment is 7 years old, but the numbers haven't been changed and I cannot find any post explaining the discrepancy.

This wouldn't be such an issue except that

the obelisk itself was carved approximately 48,9██ years ago

unidentified scripts …… with carving dates ranging from 48,███ years ago to ██ years ago

explicitly contradicts

Testing has revealed the dates of the carving of different messages to correspond with the approximate dating provided by the different iterations of the message

Legatus Maximus Romulus, 53,5██ BCE

Which means not only was the first message written by someone who lived ~6600 years before the obelisk was made, but the Foundation measured the age of the carving to be older than the obelisk itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 13,███ BCE one is Gallifreyan, calling it now lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There´s so much work put into this SCP it is almost impossible not to want to give the author credit for their work. Not to mention the article successfully hooks the reader with such an interesting topic.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So much this! (sorry for the necro)

The inscription allegedly from Victoria is dated to Victorian England. The inscription allegedly from Stalin is from 1943. The inscription allegedly from Romulus is dated to… tens of thousands of years before the agricultural revolution? Wha?

I spent the rest of the article looking for an explanation for this, or at least for an acknowledgement that it was anomalous. It seems too blatant to be a typo — five digits instead of three is pretty noticeable — but it seems like if it were intentional, someone in the Foundation would have noticed (again, because its so blatant) and commented on it, even if they were as baffled as I am.

I read through all the comments looking for clarification on whether or not this was intentional, and the "alternative assessment" Hornby linked above, and all the comments on that, but nothing.

The fact that only three comments even mention the discrepancy (and if any of the others point out specifically the problem with the founder of Rome supposedly existing so far into the prehistoric past, I missed it) is as bizarre as everything else about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great! I especially like how the validity of the inscription on the obelisk is opened to interpretation, and of course the reference to the pattern-screamers.

One small thing I noticed, however, was that the final, "predictive" date (13 599 945 679) presents a minor incongruity with its predecessor: Date 13 599 896 703 is stated to have occurred approx. 50,000 years prior to the date at which this document was published, which would at minimum would set this last date as having already happened 1024 years before the current date, which I calculated (with the aid of my personal calculator, and verified said calculations using the calculator available on Google) to be 13 599 946 703.

Oh, also, I'm giving this a +1, love the premise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this SCP. I know people will compare the Destroyers to the Reapers, but the Reapers are only one example out of many others in this archetype. The Destroyers are just our version of that archetype.

Personally, I do feel that the name of the SCP should be "Beware the Destroyers." That is the line most people associate with the SCP, rather than the obelisk itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So what are each of the scripts?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it just me or is the 5th image at the end of this SCP, the one with all the circles…. based on the Gallifreyan Language the Time Lords use from Doctor Who?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love this one. Very good Lovecraftian horror with the Destroyers, and all the work that has been put into it…
This is one of my favorites now. Congratulations.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Possibly Stalin was selected because the USSR was so large. Several of the other leaders may have also been selected on that criteria, such as Victoria and the British Empire. I imagine that it would be hard for a race of ancient aliens to determine the relative political power of human rulers in the distant future, so territory controlled was probably selected as a reasonable landmark.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I figure the message would change if the Destroyers actually showed up on a given planet, therefore providing a warning to other planets with radio technology. How useful that warning actually is depends on how fast the Destroyers can travel.

Also, are the Pattern Screamers the Destroyers or the obelisks? I dig it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1, Author, would you mind if people cross-linked this for explaining translations in other Skips? I've had a idea for a skip from Mesoamerica, but the translation has been one of my stumbling blocks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something about this one rubs me the wrong way, possibly the conflict between tone of something that apparently beowulf quotes and then a massive string of (base 8?) numbers with no explanation conflicting for me. I also don't understand how it kept generating tons of leaders, stopped at someone who I assume was a viking, and then did two modern leaders within a century of each other. Plus I'm side-eyeing stalin as a choice for a whole host of reasons, not the least of which is the fact that the USSR completely collapsed. He was a notable person of the time, but his being in charge of russia for a decade doesn't seem significant in the same way of the other leaders?

I may be having trouble with my history, and I haven't studied the history of russia around the revolution and subsequent collapse as much as I've been forced to learn minutiae of american history in school, so I could be wrong, but to me Stalin is a real weird figure to pick after a roman emperor, a viking leader, and queen Victoria.

-1 for now, wish I had a more constructive/clear way to explain why I dislike it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Always enjoyed this one, extremely in depth implications.

item.title: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Noting that the edit made with regards to "Philological plausibility" has been reverted, as it was a substantial change that altered the meaning of the article content. The author has stated that "The unusual age of the writing is a key part of the anomaly", and as such, the edit was unnecessary from a narrative standpoint as well.

Readers, as per the site rules:

You may make minor edits for grammar or spelling to other peoples' articles. Please fill out the 'Short description of changes' box. Major edits require permission from the original author or staff. Stylistic and tonal edits are considered major edits.

For this article, please post a comment regarding proposed changes rather than making the changes without permission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of Grant Morrison's Seven Soldiers. That's a good thing, mind you. It's a good concept that you've pulled off very well.

item.title: SCP-1050
item.content:encoded:
Translated to Korean wiki. (Link)

Thanks to Hornby for writing such nice historic-geographic-linguistic SCP, and thanks to foundation personnels — including two men above — who helped my translation a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The second one is closer. "People who were wise enough (to understand the threat) attempted to run away; the only people who actually survived did so through sheer luck".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
User from the Korean Wiki. What does the sentence

The wise flee Them; the lucky escape.

actually mean among those two words below?

  1. The escape, made by wise people who flew Them, was with luck.
  2. Some wise people attempted to run away, but only people with luck could made the escape.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How were you able to create the little tablet-script things?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP and Mass Effect crossover. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Uncovering and then suppressing the translation of Linear A" is going on my list of really objectionable things the Foundation has done in mainlist articles, along with "bulldozing a poor neighborhood" and "arranging the extinction of a species of whale".

It's getting special mention for not being anything they had to do to maintain containment, just something they did to keep their own secrets.

Well done. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Souchang

Uh… unlike the rest of the gods, Guan Yu (Souchang is his personal name)1 is a real person. Does that mean that the Guan Yu we know from history is an avatar for the deity Souchang?

Footnotes
1. Chinese names are traditionally complicated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, I don't really care for the whole 'researchers' notes' addenda, and noticed a few other potential tone and wording issues, but all those image and glorious, glorious footnotes really show the care put into this one. I had a damn good time reading it, and even though I don't know bupkis about history, I would say that the inclusion of octal numbering is pretty smooth, even chill.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this so much. As a language nerd, this makes me very, very happy. +1 all the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That would mean that the obelisks on other planets are using the length of Earth years to generate the numbers.

The broadcast could have an anomalous property in that it changes the year length to generate numbers depending on what planet is receiving them then the numbers would make sense for whatever planet is receiving them. Not sure how the foundation would find out about this though unless they went to mars to receive the message or something like that.

Yes I know its a old post, and no I don't care

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: By far the best SCP I've ever read. I've never had so many goosebumps and never had (or ever will have I think) such an extreme [REDACTED]. If you know what I mean.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *Clap clap clap* I would upvote just for the obvious and MASSIVE research you had to perform to write this article. But I will also upvote because it's awesome. It somehow reminds me of the Reapers from Mass Effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was a great read. +1 for the Mass Effect Reaper vibe. Nothing like the continued rise and fall of countless civilizations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To address your first confusion, the writing changes over time; e.g., the bit about Stalin wasn't there until 1943, when it seemingly appeared from nowhere (the Nazis were abhorrent in a great many ways, but they did keep good records). The mechanism by which the new text appears (or how apparently authentic copies of the proclamation appear in government archives) is something the Foundation has not been able to explain.

With respect to your primary criticism regarding the 'relative strengths' line, consider that the viewpoint of the message's original author is not at all omniscient. There isn't any evidence to suggest that the society/societies described had anything mechanical - even something as elementary as the wheel (though they are mentioned to have boats). Taken at face value, they were probably in their Iron Age, which means technology potentially no more recent than that of ~1300 BCE. If I pulled even a well-educated scholar from that time period, how would they describe something as mundane (to us) as a helicopter or truck? For that matter, what would someone contemporary to the first world war make of a modern computer? Further, we don't know when the text was written in relation to the described events: Plato's description of Atlantis is based on a dialogue between two individuals hundreds of years before when he was writing, about events allegedly 9000 years before his writing. Historians therefore take pretty much all of his account with a metric ton of salt. What if a similarly unreliable narrator was the source of this message? So, while the author is describing something which to him is incomprehensibly powerful, it may not be at all, by modern standards. There just isn't enough (any) corroborating evidence to know one way or the other.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was a little confused as to whether the writing on the obelisk changed with time or was always there.

I do like the inclusion of multiple mythic traditions and the selection of culture heroes is pretty awesome, as was their incorporation into a singular monomyth reflecting not humanity's collective unconscious but instead a real sequence of events. Conflicting historical records and mythical records (creating an anomalous timescale) is interesting but can also be taken as a weakening point in that is further disconnects the reality put forth by the SCP from the real world, though for the most part it was done well enough that I didn't really mind.

The "reaper" like aspects drew me in considering that Mass Effect handled the idea better than anybody else. I really thought this was going to be one of those rare instances where a pseudo-Lovecraftian Chaoskamf is done right, but I ended up disappointed anyway.

+ Why?
- Why

Destroyers are to the gods as the gods are to men and men are to insects

THIS is what I always have a problem with. Every time someone wants to pull out the threat of primordial chaos seeking to once again devour the universe and return to a state of utter nothingness, everybody always wants to make the "outer gods" way more powerful than "our gods."

Calling the great deities that define our universe weaklings makes it sound like the kind of hopeless wailing that makes all Lovecraft characters feel like those babbling eschatological morons on the sidewalk. It's a crude, unsupported assertion which only exists in the proclaimer's mind and thus feels devoid of value.

This is a completely backward philosophy considering that the whole story of Creation is the idea of a meaningful universe arising from the formless void. In the chaoskamf motif, the cosmological chaos comes back in the form of a dragon or something but the gods and culture-heroes of the defined universe are always triumphant. Every day our universe doesn't collapse into quantum foam, every morning Ra and Set kill Apep. In every instant since the beginning of time existence has triumphed over nothingness. We win.


The confused cosmology would usually earn a big -1 vote, but the multi-cultural synthesis gets major points, so it breaks even.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What about Eastern leaders, like Genghis Kahn, or Shi Huangdi (much apologies for any misspellings)? There are plenty of not-European people that would be recognized by the users of this wiki who controlled fairly large chunks of the planet.
Frickin' love this skip either way, though. Chills down my spine every time I read it, man. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think you and I disagree on just how widely distributed any SCP document is.1 I don't think any of the SCP articles on the main list would simply be available for any old person to read at their leisure, meaning this document is neither "public" nor something "anyone can see". The extent anyone not actively working on researching (or overseeing) 1050 would need to know is "it's a big piece of rock that needs to be kept in a (silo-sized) box which may or may not need to be able to block radio waves". As stated in the second addendum, the O5 Council has gone back and forth a couple of times on whether or not to block the transmission, specifically because of debates about the possible origin - thereby making that debate not merely an academic exercise.

Also, the speculations by the research team are only educated guesses. They may well be wrong (and, speaking as the author, in at least some respects they are). I worked rather hard to differentiate what the Foundation knows versus what it thinks. One of my personal critiques of far too many articles is that information is stated authoritatively as fact when the Foundation would have had no method by which to gain that information (and, indeed, may have had no cause to speculate in that particular direction either).

Footnotes
1. This is fine; my background is the intelligence world, not the natural sciences. Need-to-know tends to trump need-to-share by default.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it, but I thought the notes on how the obelisk might be some kind of early warning system was a bit out of character for the Foundation. You shouldn't need to overtly explain what should have been apparent from reading the article. The Foundation is legendarily close-mouthed, and I didn't picture them going and speculating on possible origins and uses of the obelisk on a public SCP document anyone can see. Its Secure, Contain, Protect, not Secure, Contain, Explain. Still upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bloody hell! I shall upvote just for all that Base-10 octal script!

Everything was good though, especially all the language research.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Me gusta. +1.

Though, a few things did jump out at me.

  • Emperor Hadrian's name was Publius Aelius Hadrianus Augustus, unless you found some source listing it as Caesar Publius Aelius Traianus Hadriani Augusti. Mostly confused by the "Hadriani Augusti" part—it's like taking John Smith and turning his name into "John Smith's".
  • "Legatus Maximus Romulus, 53,5██ BCE ("Proto-Latin" with alternate script)." For one, this predates the general consensus on when Proto-Indo-European (from which Latin descended) was spoken, around the mid fourth millenium BCE. Second, and I admittedly don't have a reference on hand to check this, it's probably a safe bet that proto-Latin was spoken around 1000 BCE at the latest, so 53,5██ BCE seems a bit much. Especially considering that, assuming there's no anomalous stuff at hand with the timescale/timeline, we wouldn't be able to recognize a language from that long ago as being related to anything, unless we had continuous documentation from then to now as the language changed.

As a disclaimer, neither of these issues is particularly make-or-break for me, and I'm aware of the possibility of one account of history or the other (i.e., the inscription's or ours) being wrong/inaccurate, etc etc. But I kinda want there to be some note about the discrepancy in Hadrian's name (maybe as simple as just "[sic]") and the anachronism of the proto-Latin date.

These two points aside, though, I loved it. I think it's a neat entry, and provides some interesting food for thought as to why those particular world leaders—why Stalin and not, say, Churchill?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I could be wrong here, but one of the carvings looks a lot like Gallifreyan script :D

http://scp-wiki.wdfiles.com/local--files/scp-1050/scp1050_1image12_zoom.gif

To give a good comparison, here is an example of Gallifreyan font:

http://www.theflyper.com/gigsimg/1304140708gallifreyan%20message%20gig.jpg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I myself like the idea that something weird is going on, and the whole obelisk isn't what it seems.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was aware of the point you're raising when I wrote the article, actually.

You're assuming that both the records which document the historical Guan Yu and the article's footnotes which "provide conjectural annotations by the research team" (specifically note 14, which is affixed to the name "Souchang") refer to the same people. There are five possibilities:

  1. You are correct, and someone who wouldn't be born for another forty-odd thousand years was defeated by the Destroyers (in other words, both the historical record and the research team are correct). (It should be noted, then, that some or all the other mentioned legendary individuals who had historical counterparts were defeated thousands of years before they were born.)
  2. The historical records is wrong (wouldn't be the first time in the SCP-verse), and the deified Chinese general lived a lot earlier than folks like you and me think.
  3. The Foundation's research team is wrong (also wouldn't be a first), and conflating a name referring to someone who lived fifty thousand years ago with someone who live much more recently (the historical Guan Yu).
  4. Both this historical record and the research team are wrong about the figure[s] of Guan Yu, for whatever reason.
  5. The article is completely wrong in its analysis, and there's something even weirder going on with the obelisk.

Depending on whether you want a Watsonian or Doylist perspective, I'd rule out numbers four and five, either as being highly improbable, given Occam's Razor (Watsonian perspective), or because those weren't narrative strategies I sought to use, as doing so would require a lot more mind-bending work to pull off well (Doylist).

Given that, numbers one, two, and three remain. You can draw your own conclusions as to which is correct (and I intended that there be a correct answer of those three choices), though I'd observe that of those three, the possibility you suggest would require a larger number of correct premises than its alternatives.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I realised something: you know how the obelisk mentions Guan Yu as one of the gods that tried to defeat the Destroyers? Well, Guan Yu was only born in the late second century, and if every version of the message is identical except for who issued it, that would mean that the mention of Guan Yu would be there even in the versions before his birth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First thought: "motherfucking reapers!"

this is fantastically well done, and deeply researched. I love the level of detail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually have the opposite problem. I tend to think of the SCPs solely in terms of their numeric designation, and not think about or remember their "names."

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: We can do without the sarcastic response. Honestly, I'm not sure why you felt the need to reply to someones opinion, or so harshly, but it's not needed, mmkay?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Open snarky/sarcastic rant-response-thing XD
- Close snarly/sarcastic rant-response-thing

> All that effort doesn't have a single hook or original thought to show for itself!

Hey, look! Opinion pretending to be fact!

And I don't come here for math or history, I come here for creepy/unnerving/WTF-inducing works of art.

So you state opinion as fact, then point out how you're just voicing your opinion… but that opinion is fact, so we better listen, eh? So you come here for those reasons, and this article doesn't do it for you? Cool. That happens, but something doesn't get to +200 if the majority having read it doesn't genuinely like it for whatever reason(s). Just because you don't like it doesn't mean it's bad.

Okay, maybe I'm getting too worked up about this.

Yep. =)

As an article specific to this site, it's bad. Downvoted.

More opinion presented as fact. There are plenty of articles on the site that aren't "creepy/unnerving/WTF-inducing", and there's a good number that suck, and a good number that are good. I haven't seen a rule anywhere that says "make your SCP creepy or it'll be automatically deleted." Though it's definitely a rule you can upvote or down vote something as you see fit.

Anyway, I personally liked this specifically because it made me think of Mass Effect. Love those games. Others dislike it for the same reason, and it's cool we can have different opinions based on the same facts. But that's just what they are: opinions we each have. =)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hovering on the footnotes helps. I'm usually on a tablet, and don't want to scroll that much. I just put my finger on the footnote and slide it a little so it doesn't move me down and a little box appears with the info. Very convenient.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you send me the background image and the font HEX colour, I can translate it to Russian.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "They come by the millions from the Realm of Darkness which extends where no stars shine". Should be somehow connected with Smile Man from SCP-507 and Dream Man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have an army.
We have a Clef.

Anyway, this is one of the few Safe SCPs that makes me shiver. All those references… all those heroes and gods that stood and failed. It's just epic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to like this, especially given the incredible amount of effort and research prior commenters have made note of that went into this article…but I don't. It reminds me of "He Who Made Dark" (I can never remember any but the absolute most well-known SCPs by their numbers), only somehow less ominous and overall less interesting. Mysterious writing out of nowhere? Done. Predictions of some form of "Destroyer" entities? Done. Mysterious broadcast messages written in math? Overdone. The most interesting/original thing about this article is the signal bonfire note, and that's actual real history, barely even tangentially related to the SCP; personally, I don't see how this thing could perform such a function, given that it's constantly broadcasting the same signal in spite of there being no apparent danger when the signal is broadcast.

Unlike the bone-eating owl, the more I think about this (and discuss it in a comment), the less I like it. Again, I do have to acknowledge the amount of detail in this article, and I truly respect all the research, math, and general effort put into this, really I do…but it just isn't very interesting. I can see why some people would upvote it for the effort alone, but over +200? Really? All that effort doesn't have a single hook or original thought to show for itself! And yes, I know that most things have been done before and something being "unoriginal" doesn't necessarily make it bad, but this article is…basically a mosaic of some of the least-interesting bits of other SCPs, all pasted together with admittedly impressive amounts of math and history and such. And I don't come here for math or history, I come here for creepy/unnerving/WTF-inducing works of art.

Okay, maybe I'm getting too worked up about this. The point I'm trying to make is that the math and history and research is impressive, but the SCP itself is anything but, and I could learn that math or history on websites completely unlike our beloved SCP Foundation Wiki. As an article specific to this site, it's bad. Downvoted.

(As a side note, I know next to nothing about Mass Effect, Stargate: Atlantis, or any of the other franchises mentioned in these comments, so the feeling of redundancy I'm getting doesn't stem from my comparing the article to any of those.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: USSR didn't have a president until Gorbacov. Fail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty certain premier referred to the president which would have been Kalinin

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Never enough footnotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You went way overboard on footnotes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a love/hate relationship with these super long detailed ones.

Impressive as hell though +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be fair, there's the mysteriously appearing carvings, but that doesn't have to be magic - it could just be advanced technology.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great, I especially love the paragraph devoted to the Prisoner's Dilemna-esque problem of whether to pass on the message or not- it's both interesting to ponder and moves the whole piece back into the real world a little.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oi, cut the sig.

Also, never too many footnotes.
